- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

1	# Unit F: injective fact instances
2	
3	Build crate injfacts-clean in workspace/. The binary computes which protocol
4	facts are "injective" during precomputation; `oracle/hs_oracle.sh <file>
5	--precompute-only` prints the precomputation — find where injective facts
6	appear in that output (and/or in normal output/web captures) and use it as
7	your labeled oracle. Characterize the decision: for each fact tag, when is it
8	injective (probe: rules where a fact is created with a fresh name, consumed,
9	re-produced, multiple instances, etc. — design minimal probe theories).
10	Input model: rules from ../wellformedness/interface/ast_types.rs. Deliverable:
11	injective_fact_instances(rules) -> set of fact tags, corpus-validated against
12	oracle/examples/ (37 theories) plus your probes; byte-match any printed
13	representation you rely on. BEHAVIOR.md + QUERIES.log + tests; REPORT.md +
14	10-line summary.
15

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Isolated per-theory injective scraper.
set -u
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
CORP=$SP/corpus
ORACLE=/home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/injective/oracle/hs_oracle.sh
OUT=$SP/corpus_results.tsv
LOG=$SP/driver.log
BUDGET=${BUDGET:-200}          # seconds per theory
: > "$OUT"; : > "$LOG"
port=3500
while IFS=$'\t' read -r uid rel; do
  port=$((port+1))
  dir="$CORP/$uid"
  slog="$SP/srvlog_$uid.txt"
  echo "[$(date +%T)] START $uid ($rel) port $port" >> "$LOG"
  ORACLE_TIMEOUT=$BUDGET "$ORACLE" interactive "$dir" --port=$port --no-logging > "$slog" 2>&1 &
  spid=$!
  # wait for readiness: index page lists a /thy/ link, OR server died
  ready=0
  for i in $(seq 1 $BUDGET); do
    if ! kill -0 "$spid" 2>/dev/null; then break; fi
    idx=$(curl -s -m 3 "http://127.0.0.1:$port/" 2>/dev/null)
    if echo "$idx" | grep -q "/thy/"; then ready=1; break; fi
    sleep 1
  done
  if [ "$ready" = 1 ]; then
    res=$(python3 "$SP/scrape_one.py" "$port" 2>>"$LOG")
    echo -e "$uid\t$res" >> "$OUT"
    echo "[$(date +%T)] DONE  $uid -> $res" >> "$LOG"
  else
    echo -e "$uid\tLOAD_FAIL_OR_TIMEOUT" >> "$OUT"
    echo "[$(date +%T)] FAIL  $uid (not ready in ${BUDGET}s)" >> "$LOG"
  fi
  kill "$spid" 2>/dev/null
  pkill -P "$spid" 2>/dev/null
  # ensure port freed
  for j in $(seq 1 5); do kill -0 "$spid" 2>/dev/null || break; sleep 1; done
  kill -9 "$spid" 2>/dev/null
done < "$CORP/MANIFEST.tsv"
echo "[$(date +%T)] ALL DONE" >> "$LOG"

# BEHAVIOR.md — Unit F: injective fact instances All statements here are derived from oracle observations (interactive web UI `/thy/.../main/rules` page, section "Fact Symbols with Injective Instances") and the input model `ast_types.rs`. No tamarin-prover source was read. ## Where the oracle surfaces the answer The CLI (`--precompute-only`, `-m …`, `--verbose`) never prints the word "injective" as a fact property — it appears only as lemma names. The labeled oracle is the **interactive web UI**: - Launch: `hs_oracle.sh interactive --port=P --no-logging` (note: `--port=` must use `=`; a bare `--port P` makes `P` the positional WORKDIR). - The page `/thy/trace//main/rules` (AJAX, returns JSON `{"html": …}`) begins with, exact bytes: `

Fact Symbols with Injective Instances


\n

…

` where `…` is either `None` or a comma-space-separated, **name-sorted** list of fact-instance templates, e.g. `MB(id), ZA(id)`. - Diff theories are served under `/thy/equiv//…` instead of `/thy/trace/…`. ### Rendering of one injective template `F( a0, a1, … )` renders with the fresh first-argument variable shown by its base name (`~id` → `id`) and every non-first position shown as a placeholder (`=` when that position is a bare variable, `.` when it is/varies as a non-variable). Only the **first argument** participates in the injective key; the placeholder for the other positions is cosmetic. Our deliverable extracts the fact **name + arity** (the tag) from this render; we do not reproduce the placeholder glyph. ## The decision (characterised from 35 minimal probes, then corpus-validated) Let the protocol rules be the input. For a fact **tag** `t = (name, arity)` that is **linear** (not persistent `!`), define per rule `r`: - `premKeys(t,r)` = multiset of first arguments of `t`-facts in `r.premises` - `concKeys(t,r)` = multiset of first arguments of `t`-facts in `r.conclusions` - `freshVars(r)` = multiset of variables `v` such that a linear fact `Fr(v)` occurs in `r.premises` `t` is **injective** iff BOTH: - **(I) State loop.** There exists a rule where `t` occurs in *both* premises and conclusions (same rule). - **(II) Conservative creation.** For *every* rule `r`, the multiset difference `netNew = concKeys(t,r) ⊖ premKeys(t,r)` is a **sub-multiset of** `freshVars(r)`. I.e. every `t`-conclusion whose first argument is not cancelled by a `t`-premise with the same first argument must be a distinct freshly-generated (`Fr`-bound) variable. Persistent facts, and any tag that never appears in both a premise and a conclusion of one rule, are excluded. Reserved facts (`Fr`, `In`, `Out`, `K`, `KU`, `KD`) are excluded automatically by (I): none occurs in both premise and conclusion of a single rule. Arity-0 facts have no first argument and are never injective (guarded explicitly). ## Evidence grid (probe → observed → rule branch that explains it) | probe | rule shape (fact F) | observed | explanation | |---|---|---|---| | p01 | Fr→F(~id); F(~id)→F(~id); F(~id)→· | F(id) | (I)✓ loop; (II)✓ | | p02 | Fr→F(~id); F(~id)→· (no loop) | None | (I)✗ no same-rule loop | | p03 | Fr→!F(~id) (persistent) | None | persistent excluded | | p04 | In(x)→F(x); F(x)→F(x) | None | (II)✗ producer key x not fresh/carried | | p05 | Fr→F('c',~id); loop | None | first arg is 'c' (pub const), not fresh | | p06 | [Fr~a,Fr~b]→[F~a,F~b]; F(~x)→· | None | (I)✗ no loop | | p07 | Fr→F(~id); [F(~id),Fr~id2]→F(~id2) | F(id) | (I)✓; (II)✓ (~id2 fresh) | | p08 | ·→F('a'); F(x)→· | None | first arg const; (I)✗ | | p09 | Fr→F(~id) AND In(x)→F(x); F(x)→· | None | (II)✗ (In producer) & (I)✗ | | p11 | [Fr~id,In x]→F(~id,x); loop; consume | F(id,=) | (I)✓; (II)✓ only 1st arg matters | | b01 | [F(~id),Fr~id2]→[F(~id),F(~id2)] +Init | F(id) | (II)✓ 1 carried + 1 fresh | | b02 | 2 producers (both Fr), 1 consumer, no loop | None | (I)✗ | | b03 | Fr→F(~id); #!/usr/bin/env bash
set -u
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
CORP=$SP/corpus
ORACLE=/home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/injective/oracle/hs_oracle.sh
OUT=$SP/corpus_results.tsv
HTML=$SP/corpus_html
LOG=$SP/driver2.log
BUDGET=${BUDGET:-150}
mkdir -p "$HTML"
: > "$OUT"; : > "$LOG"
port=3600
while IFS=$'\t' read -r uid rel; do
  port=$((port+1))
  dir="$CORP/$uid"; slog="$SP/srv2_$uid.txt"
  echo "[$(date +%T)] START $uid port $port" >> "$LOG"
  ORACLE_TIMEOUT=$BUDGET "$ORACLE" interactive "$dir" --port=$port --no-logging > "$slog" 2>&1 &
  spid=$!
  ready=0
  for i in $(seq 1 $BUDGET); do
    kill -0 "$spid" 2>/dev/null || break
    if curl -s -m 3 "http://127.0.0.1:$port/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "/thy/"; then ready=1; break; fi
    sleep 1
  done
  if [ "$ready" = 1 ]; then
    res=$(python3 "$SP/scrape_save.py" "$port" "$HTML/$uid.html" 2>>"$LOG")
    echo -e "$uid\t$res" >> "$OUT"
    echo "[$(date +%T)] DONE  $uid -> $res" >> "$LOG"
  else
    echo -e "$uid\tLOAD_FAIL_OR_TIMEOUT" >> "$OUT"
    echo "[$(date +%T)] FAIL  $uid" >> "$LOG"
  fi
  kill "$spid" 2>/dev/null; pkill -P "$spid" 2>/dev/null
  for j in $(seq 1 4); do kill -0 "$spid" 2>/dev/null || break; sleep 1; done
  kill -9 "$spid" 2>/dev/null
  pkill -9 -f "port=$port" 2>/dev/null
done < "$CORP/MANIFEST.tsv"
echo "[$(date +%T)] ALL DONE" >> "$LOG"

# Unit F — Injective Fact Instances: clean-room report ## 1. Mandate Reimplement, without access to GPL sources, the precomputation that decides which protocol facts have *injective instances*. Deliverable: a function `injective_fact_instances(rules) -> set of fact tags` over the parsed-theory data model in `ast_types.rs`, corpus-validated against the oracle and probe theories. Everything below is derived exclusively from (a) black-box oracle observations, (b) the provided interface header, and (c) this unit's SPEC. No file under `/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/` was read except by executing the `oracle/hs_oracle.sh` wrapper; no tamarin-prover source, docs, or internals from memory were used. ## 2. Finding the labeled oracle The CLI never prints injectivity: `--precompute-only`, `--verbose`, and every `-m` output module were searched — "injective" appears only as a *lemma name*. The property is surfaced only by the **interactive web UI**. Launching `hs_oracle.sh interactive --port=

--no-logging` and requesting `/thy/trace//main/rules` returns JSON whose HTML body opens with, byte-for-byte: ```

Fact Symbols with Injective Instances


…

``` `…` is `None`, or a `, `-separated, **name-sorted** list of fact templates such as `Sqn_HSS(id,=,<,=,<), Sqn_UE(id,=,≤,=,≤)`. In a template the fresh first-argument variable is shown by name (`~id` → `id`) and every other position is a cosmetic placeholder (`=`, `.`, `<`, `≤`, …) that does not affect the tag. Our deliverable returns fact **tags** (name + arity); the placeholder glyphs are not reproduced. Diff theories are served under `/thy/equiv//…`. ## 3. The decision (characterised, not recalled) A **linear** fact tag `t` (arity ≥ 1, never persistent) has injective instances **iff both**: - **(I) State loop.** Some rule contains `t` in *both* its premises and its conclusions. - **(II) Conservative creation.** For *every* rule, let `premKeys`/`concKeys` be the multisets of first arguments of `t`-facts in the premises/conclusions, and `freshVars` the variables generated by that rule's `Fr(_)` premises. Then `concKeys ⊖ premKeys` (multiset difference by term equality) must be a **sub-multiset of** `freshVars`: every net-new instance carries a *distinct freshly generated* name in its first argument. Equivalently: `t` is a linear "state thread" keyed by a fresh name in argument 0, never duplicated and only ever created from `Fr`. ### Why each clause is there (falsifiable evidence) - Persistent facts are never injective (`p03`). - Without a same-rule loop, even a fresh-created + separately-consumed fact is *not* injective (`p02`, `b02`): (I) is independently necessary. - Only the **first** argument matters (`p05`: fresh in position 2 → None; `p11`,`e01`: other positions free). - The key must be a **fresh variable**, not a pub/const/function term (`b05`,`b06`,`p04`,`p08`). - A carried key must come from a **premise of the same tag** (`c06`,`d05` pass; `d01`,`d02` from `In`/another fact → None). - A newly created key must be **`Fr`-generated**, not merely fresh-*sorted* (`d04`: fresh var from a persistent premise → None). - Creation is **counted**: consuming one and producing two copies of the same key breaks it (`e02`,`e03`,`g02`), while carry-through + one new fresh is fine (`b01`,`b07`,`g01`). The full 35-probe evidence grid is in `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md`; each probe is reproduced as an AST fixture unit test in `tests/probes.rs`. ## 4. Implementation `workspace/injfacts-clean/` (crate `injfacts-clean`): - `src/ast.rs` — the provided interface header (data model only; no logic). - `src/lib.rs` — `injective_fact_instances(&[Rule]) -> BTreeSet<(String,usize)>` implementing (I)+(II) directly over `premises`/`conclusions`. - `src/bin/injfacts.rs` — a thin harness: reconstructs `Rule`/`Fact`/`Term` values from a compact line format and calls the library, used to validate the real function end-to-end on rules parsed from the oracle's own normalized rule pretty-print. - `tests/probes.rs` — 32 fixtures (all probes + an arity-0 guard); all pass. ## 5. Validation Two independent levels: 1. **Probe fixtures (AST-level).** All 32 tests pass — the function reproduces every labeled probe. 2. **Corpus end-to-end.** `scratchpad/validate.py` parses each theory's rendered MSR rules from `/main/rules` into the compact format, runs the compiled `injfacts` binary, and compares its output to the oracle's injective line on the same page. This exercises the real library on the actual (post-translation) rules the oracle used. ### Corpus results The most substantive positive case is the CCS'18 5G-AKA models, where the oracle and our implementation independently agree on the injective set `{Sqn_HSS/5, Sqn_UE/5}` — arity-5 sequence-number state facts keyed by a fresh subscriber id. ## 6. Scope notes - The function consumes a `Rule` list. For **SAPIC** theories the AST carries processes, not rules; the caller must pass the translated MSR rules (the oracle computes over exactly those, which is what our corpus harness parses from the render). For **diff** theories the caller supplies the concrete left/right rules. - Reserved facts (`Fr`,`In`,`Out`,`K`,`KU`,`KD`) and intruder rules never yield false positives: they are persistent or never form a linear same-rule loop, so clause (I) excludes them without special-casing.
